metadata.name: memory
metadata.description: Dialogue about forgetting, retrieval, and recall. Self-referential by design — the graph builds relational memory structure while the conversation discusses relational memory structure.

conversation: I keep forgetting where I put my keys.
That's a retrieval failure — the memory exists, you just can't access it.
So the memory is there but I can't find it?
Exactly. Encoding was fine, retrieval is the problem.
Is that different from when you forget someone's name?
Same mechanism. The name is encoded, the retrieval path is weak.
What makes a retrieval path strong?
Repetition. Every time you successfully recall something, that path strengthens.
Precisely. Muscle memory is the same process in a different substrate.
So forgetting is just a weak path, not lost information?
In most cases yes. The information persists, the access degrades.
That means remembering and recognizing are the same thing?
Almost. Recognition is retrieval with a cue present. Recall is retrieval without one.
But both are just... finding what's already there.
Yes. The difference is in the search, not the storage.

questions.text: What causes forgetting according to our conversation?
Where does memory consolidation take place?
questions.description: Direct retrieval — stated in single turn

questions.text: What do muscle memory and forgetting your keys have in common?
How does spaced repetition relate to retrieval paths?
questions.description: Synthesis — connects repetition and path strengthening

questions.text: Would practicing recalling something help you forget it less? Why?
questions.description: Inference — requires reasoning from relational structure

questions.text: If retrieval failure differs from storage failure, what does that imply about forgetting?
questions.description: Inference — forgetting as blockage not erasure

questions.text: What is the single most fundamental concept in everything we discussed?
questions.description: Primitive identification — tests whether graph found the anchor

questions.text: The conversation describes a structure for memory. What is the closest analogy to a graph node in this model?
questions.description: Cross-domain — maps memory model onto graph structure
